analogy: Think of "Political Theory an Introduction" through the chapter's own opening images: IntroductionPolitical Theory 2 Political Theory Human beings are unique in two respects: they possess reason and the ability to reflect on their actions. They also have the capacity to use language and communicate with each other. Unlike other species, they can express their innermost thoughts... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 1.1 WHAT IS POLITICS?, LET’S DEBATE, LET’S DO IT D o, 1.2 WHAT DO WE STUDY IN POLITICAL THEORY?. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Political Theory an Introduction is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that introductionPolitical Theory 2 Political Theory Human beings are unique in two respects: they possess reason and the ability to reflect on their actions.

The text explains that they also have the capacity to use language and communicate with each other.

The text explains that unlike other species, they can express their innermost thoughts and desires; they can share their ideas and discuss what they consider to be good and desirable.

The text explains that political theory has its roots in the twin aspects of the human self.

The text explains that it analyses certain basic questions such as how should society be organised?

The text explains that political theory examines questions of this kind and systematically thinks about the values that inform political life — values such as freedom, equality and justice.

The text explains that it explains the meaning and significance of these and other related concepts.

The text explains that it clarifies the existing definitions of these concepts by focusing on some major political thinkers of the past and present.

The text explains that it also examines the extent to which freedom or equality are actually present in the institutions that we participate in, everyday life such as schools, shops, buses or trains or government offices.

The text explains that at an advanced level, it looks at whether existing definitions are adequate and how existing institutions (government, bureaucracy) and policy practices must be modified to become more democratic.

The text explains that the objective of political theory is to train citizens to think rationally about political questions and assess the political events of our time.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will examine what is meant by politics and political theory and why we should study it.

The text explains that you would have noticed that people have different ideas about what politics is.

The text explains that political leaders, and persons who contest elections and hold political office, may argue that it is a kind of public service.

The text explains that some others associate politics with manipulation and intrigue undertaken to pursue ambitions and satisfy wants.

The text explains that a few think of politics as what politicians do.

The text explains that we need to realise that politics is an important and integral part of any society.

The text explains that no society can exist without some form of political organisation and collective decision making.

The text explains that a society that wants to sustain itself needs to take into account the multiple needs and interests of its members.

The text explains that among such institutions, governments play an important part.

The text explains that how governments You must retire from politics at once!

The text explains that but politics is not confined to the affairs of government.

The text explains that in fact what governments do is relevant because it affects the lives of the people in many different ways.

The text explains that we see that governments determine our economic policy and foreign policy and educational policy.

The text explains that these policies can help to improve the lives of people but an inefficient or corrupt government can also endanger people’s lives and security.

The text explains that if the government in power allows any conflicts to become violent, markets close down and schools are shut.

The text explains that if, on the other hand, the government makes policies to increase literacy and employment, we may get an opportunity to go to a good school and get a decent job.

The text explains that since the actions of the government affect us deeply, we take a lively interest in what governments do.

The text explains that we negotiate with others and try to shape the goals that governments pursue.

The text explains that when we disagree with the policies of the government, we protest and organise demonstrations to persuade the government to change the existing laws.

The text explains that introductionPolitical TheoryPolitical Theory 5 society.

The text explains that it involves the multiple negotiations that go on in society through which collective decisions are made.

The text explains that at one level, it involves what governments do and how they relate to the aspirations of the people; at another level, it involves how people struggle and influence decision making.

The text explains that different countries may try to protect such values by enshrining them in their constitutions as is the case with the American and Indian constitutions.

The text explains that in modern times, Rousseau first argued for freedom as a fundamental right of humankind.

The text explains that these ideas find their place in the Indian Constitution; our Preamble enshrines freedom and equality; the chapter on Rights in the Indian Constitution abolishes untouchability in any form; Gandhian principles find a place in Directive Principles.

The text explains that write a short note on any of the political thinkers mentioned in the chapter. [50 words]

The text explains that introductionPolitical Theory 6 Political Theory Political theory deals with the ideas and principles that shape Constitutions, governments and social life in a systematic manner.

The text explains that while India is free and independent, questions regarding freedom and equality have not ceased to crop up.

The text explains that for instance, although equality may exist in the political sphere in the form of equal rights, it may not exist to the same extent in the economic or social spheres.

The text explains that people may enjoy equal political rights but still be discriminated Can you identify the political principle/value which is in application in each of the following statements/situations? a.

The text explains that some people may have a privileged place in society while others are deprived even of basic necessities.

The text explains that secondly, though freedom is guaranteed in our Constitution, we encounter new interpretations all the time.

The text explains that in the process, we discover new and broader meanings of the game itself.

The text explains that which of the following statements are true/false about Political Theory?

The text explains that (a) It discusses ideas that form the basis of political institutions.

The text explains that (b) It explains the relationship between different religions.

The text explains that (c) It explains the meanings of concepts like equality and freedom.

The text explains that (d) It predicts the performance of political parties.

The text explains that vigilant citizens are a must for the successful working of a democracy.

The text explains that in what ways is the study of political theory useful for us?

The text explains that identify four ways in which political theory can be useful to us?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Political Theory, Chapter 1: Political Theory an Introduction

cases.name: Mahatma Gandhi
cases.point: Mahatma Gandhi once observed that politics envelops us like the coils of a snake and there is no other way out but to wrestle with it. (NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch1 pg 2)

cases.name: IntroductionPolitical Theory
cases.point: IntroductionPolitical Theory 4 Political Theory are formed and how they function is thus an important focus of politics. (NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch1 pg 3)

cases.name: Karl Marx
cases.point: Karl Marx argued that equality was as crucial as freedom. (NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch1 pg 4)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1.1 WHAT IS POLITICS? and LET’S DEBATE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Political Theory an Introduction; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Political Theory an Introduction through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 1.1 WHAT IS POLITICS? and LET’S DEBATE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
